***The Equalizer*** is an American [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") [crime](/wiki/Crime "Crime") [drama](/wiki/Drama_(genre) "Drama (genre)") television series, originally airing on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") from September 18, 1985, to August 24, 1989, which was co-created by Michael Sloan and Richard Lindheim. It starred [Edward Woodward](/wiki/Edward_Woodward "Edward Woodward") as Robert McCall, a retired intelligence agent with a mysterious past, who uses the skills from his former career to exact justice on behalf of innocent people who find themselves in dangerous circumstances, while sometimes also dealing with people from his past in covert operations who want to pull him back in or settle old scores. In the years after its cancellation, the show became a [cult classic](/wiki/Cult_classic "Cult classic")[[1]](#cite_note-CultRant-1) that inspired further works, including [three feature films](#Film_series) and a [re-imagined series](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(2021_TV_series) "The Equalizer (2021 TV series)").

## Series plot elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Series plot elements")]

The series stars British actor [Edward Woodward](/wiki/Edward_Woodward "Edward Woodward") as Robert McCall, a former [covert operations](/wiki/Covert_operations "Covert operations") officer of an unnamed American government intelligence organization, which was often referred to simply as "the Agency" or "the Company", who tries to atone for his past by offering, usually free of charge, his services as a troubleshooter, a protector, and an investigator.[[2]](#cite_note-Equalizer01-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Equalizer05-3)

People in need found him through a newspaper classified ad: "Got a problem? Odds against you? Call the Equalizer: 212 555 4200."[[3]](#cite_note-Equalizer05-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Equalizer04-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Equalizer02-5) When he began the business in the pilot episode, the nickname "Equalizer" was revealed as bestowed on him by an operative named "Brahms", played by [Jerry Stiller](/wiki/Jerry_Stiller "Jerry Stiller").[[3]](#cite_note-Equalizer05-3)

Aided by a group of sometimes mysterious contacts, some of whom date back to his spying days, McCall traverses the streets of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), delivering justice upon bullies, corrupt police officials and politicians, hoodlums, mobsters, rapists, racists, murderers, kidnappers, drug dealers, and other "truly deserving" people. "Please don't do anything you will never live to regret," he tells one villain.[[6]](#cite_note-Equalizer06-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

His contacts were also prone to human foibles that ranged from egoism to domestic problems.[[5]](#cite_note-Equalizer02-5)

Many episodes focused on McCall interacting with "Control" (played by [Robert Lansing](/wiki/Robert_Lansing_(actor) "Robert Lansing (actor)")), the unnamed head of the Manhattan office of the secret organization for which McCall used to work.[[3]](#cite_note-Equalizer05-3) As a general rule, however, the people responding to the newspaper ad were unremarkable, average, and unknown.[[8]](#cite_note-Equalizer03-8)[[6]](#cite_note-Equalizer06-6)

McCall's [Jaguar XJ](/wiki/Jaguar_XJ "Jaguar XJ") [saloon car](/wiki/Saloon_car "Saloon car"), weapons, and other gadgetry at times featured significantly as elements in the plot.[[5]](#cite_note-Equalizer02-5)

## Cast and characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast and characters")]

### Main

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Main")]

* [Edward Woodward](/wiki/Edward_Woodward "Edward Woodward") as Robert McCall: A veteran operative of The Company who becomes disillusioned with sacrificing ordinary people for the perceived greater good. He quits and takes out a newspaper advert offering his services to those who need it as The Equalizer. McCall himself is divorced, a "lost dad" long estranged from his son, Scott ([William Zabka](/wiki/William_Zabka "William Zabka")). Scott comes back into his life as a young adult who is at first bitterly critical of his father's world, but then becomes drawn into that world to the dismay of both of his parents. McCall also lost a woman he was in love with, a fellow operative named Manon Brevard, and discovers that she had secretly given birth to his daughter, Yvette.[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9) McCall appears to be independently wealthy, as although he almost never takes payment for his work, he owns a high-end apartment, is always well dressed and drives a [Jaguar XJ6](/wiki/Jaguar_XJ6 "Jaguar XJ6") car (registered 5809-AUG). He enjoys classical music (playing the piano himself), fine wine and dining and is occasionally seen dating and trying to live a "normal" life, only for work or his past to get in the way. His father, William, was a [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") officer who was killed in Egypt in 1952 when McCall was 19 and also an army officer. His mother was a working-class American entertainer and his father was disinherited by his family and shunned by his regiment for being seen to be marrying beneath himself.[[10]](#cite_note-EqS4E17-10) McCall had a strained relationship with his father, whom he blamed for remaining overseas and leaving him at boarding school while his mother was dying. He was also guilt-ridden at his various actions during the Cold War, notably helping to [establish the dictatorship in Chile](/wiki/Military_dictatorship_of_Chile_(1973%E2%80%931990) "Military dictatorship of Chile (1973–1990)") and working with South Africa's [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") regime in Angola. Woodward was nominated for an Emmy for Outstanding Actor in a Drama Series four years in a row but never won, and was also nominated in 1986 and 1987 for the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Television Series Drama, winning in 1986.
* [Keith Szarabajka](/wiki/Keith_Szarabajka "Keith Szarabajka") as Mickey Kostmayer (Also starring, 56 episodes): "Mickey" is short for Michael.[[11]](#cite_note-EqS4E11-11) He is introduced in the second episode of the series, "China Rain."[[12]](#cite_note-EqS1E2-12) McCall has to call in a marker with Control to enlist Mickey, after trading for McCall's notes and contacts on a Company operation. McCall finds him pier fishing. Mickey likes the idea of an Equalizer, and McCall needs "some wacko who's willing to stick his finger in the fan. So, who we helping?"[[12]](#cite_note-EqS1E2-12) He was a [Navy SEAL](/wiki/Navy_SEAL "Navy SEAL"), who was thrown in the brig for a crime he did not commit, until McCall cleared him and recommended he join The Company.[[13]](#cite_note-EqS4E6-13) He is often utilized to surveil suspects and protect clients and witnesses. Always laconic, he is from Houston, Texas, of Polish/American origin, and served in Cambodia during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), claiming to have stayed awake during one mission for 96 hours straight. He enjoys fishing in his free time, is a fan of the [Knicks basketball team](/wiki/Knicks_basketball_team "Knicks basketball team") and bubble-gum, and builds models out of matchsticks. His brother is a priest, who lives New York City, and needs the help of McCall and Mickey in one episode.[[14]](#cite_note-EqS2E10-14) While McCall is almost always dressed in a suit, Mickey is usually seen in jeans and wearing a watch cap and army overcoat. A black Dodge van is his usual mode of transport.

### Recurring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Recurring")]

* [Robert Lansing](/wiki/Robert_Lansing_(actor) "Robert Lansing (actor)") as Control (29 episodes). A contemporary of McCall's in The Company who has risen to a senior rank; he and McCall are usually friendly, although at times Control's focus on the mission causes friction with McCall's desire to avoid collateral damage. Although McCall does not always agree with Control's methods, he usually helps his old friend when younger and even more ruthless agents try to force him out. For his part Control allows McCall to utilize Company men like Mickey, Jimmy, and Sterno provided they are not on assignment. Known for his trademark bowties.
* [Mark Margolis](/wiki/Mark_Margolis "Mark Margolis") as Jimmy (16 episodes). Another Company veteran, Jimmy is an expert in surveillance, and McCall brings him in when high-tech bugs, wiretaps, tranquilizer guns, and such are required. He almost always has a story about his ex-wife to tell McCall, starting with his first appearance early in season one, in "Lady Cop." Commenting on his marital/financial trouble, "I'm going through a divorce. When she gets done with me...nothing left but a grease spot." So, he welcomes the work, "You got anything else, you let me know."[[15]](#cite_note-EqS1E5-15) Abandoned by his father at age 6, Jimmy is part of the [Big Brother](/wiki/Big_Brothers_Big_Sisters_of_New_York_City "Big Brothers Big Sisters of New York City") organization, mentoring troubled children.[[16]](#cite_note-EqS4E10-16)
* [William Zabka](/wiki/William_Zabka "William Zabka") as Scott McCall (12 episodes). McCall's estranged son when the series begins, their growing relationship is one of the recurring themes throughout the series. Scott is an accomplished musician but is drawn more and more deeply into his father's world as the series progresses. Scott also knows of the kinship between himself, Yvette, and their father although McCall has sworn him to secrecy. (In season 2, Scott meets and later dates Jenny Morrow, played by [Lori Loughlin](/wiki/Lori_Loughlin "Lori Loughlin").)
* [Chad Redding](/w/index.php?title=Chad_Redding&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chad Redding (page does not exist)") as Sgt. Alice Shepard (10 episodes). An NYPD detective in the second[[17]](#cite_note-EqS2E18-17) through fourth seasons,[[18]](#cite_note-EqS4E21-18) who often assists McCall, recognizing that he can take action where she cannot. Shepard proves to be a versatile asset who McCall first meets at [Juvenile Division](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department#Organization_and_structure "New York City Police Department"),[[17]](#cite_note-EqS2E18-17) but later transfers to [Intelligence Division](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department_Intelligence_Bureau "New York City Police Department Intelligence Bureau").[[19]](#cite_note-EqS3E5-19) After her unit is disbanded,[[20]](#cite_note-EqS3E8-20) she is temporary attached to the [Organized Crime Task Force](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department_Organized_Crime_Control_Bureau "New York City Police Department Organized Crime Control Bureau").[[21]](#cite_note-EqS3E12-21) She also liaises with colleagues from the Gang Task Force.[[22]](#cite_note-EqS4E2-22) Redding appears ten times as Shephard, and once previously as Beth Mackie[[23]](#cite_note-EqS1E22-23) in season one before her new role.[[24]](#cite_note-EqS3E22-24)[[25]](#cite_note-EqS4E7-25)[[26]](#cite_note-EqS4E13-26)[[27]](#cite_note-EqS4E18-27)
* [Richard Jordan](/wiki/Richard_Jordan "Richard Jordan") as Harley Gage (10 episodes). Another disillusioned Company veteran, Gage was brought in by Richard Dyson (played by [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum")) to track down McCall when he goes missing in "Mission: McCall." Gage ends up staying on and helping McCall with clients throughout season three.[[28]](#cite_note-EqS3E9-28)[[29]](#cite_note-EqS3E10-29)[[30]](#cite_note-EqS3E11-30)[[21]](#cite_note-EqS3E12-21)[[31]](#cite_note-EqS3E13-31)[[32]](#cite_note-EqS3E14-32) This was done to lighten the load on Woodward after he suffered a heart attack,[[33]](#cite_note-NYfamily-33) although Keith Szarabajka was featured in only four of the episodes with Jordan.[[34]](#cite_note-EqS3E6-34)[[35]](#cite_note-EqS3E7-35)[[20]](#cite_note-EqS3E8-20)[[36]](#cite_note-EqS3E15-36)
* [Maureen Anderman](/wiki/Maureen_Anderman "Maureen Anderman") as Pete O'Phelan (8 episodes). The widow of McCall's former colleague and a former operative herself, she owns *O'Phelan's* bar and restaurant that McCall and Mickey frequent, and she occasionally helps with their missions. She is introduced in the season three episode, "Christmas Presence,"[[30]](#cite_note-EqS3E11-30) and appears 6 more times in season three.[[21]](#cite_note-EqS3E12-21)[[32]](#cite_note-EqS3E14-32)[[37]](#cite_note-EqS3E19-37)[[38]](#cite_note-EqS3E20-38)[[39]](#cite_note-EqS3E21-39)[[24]](#cite_note-EqS3E22-24) Her last episode is the season four premiere, "The Last Campaign."[[40]](#cite_note-EqS4E1-40) Anderman had previously appeared as Eleanor Griffith[[41]](#cite_note-EqS1E4-41) in season one before her new role.
* [Ron O'Neal](/wiki/Ron_O%27Neal "Ron O'Neal") as Lt. Isadore Smalls (7 episodes). An NYPD detective who assists McCall beginning in "Wash-Up,"[[42]](#cite_note-EqS1E16-42) and three more times in the first season.[[43]](#cite_note-EqS1E17-43)[[44]](#cite_note-EqS1E18-44)[[23]](#cite_note-EqS1E22-23) His last appearance is in season two, "Nightscape."[[45]](#cite_note-EqS2E2-45)[[46]](#cite_note-EqS2E4-46)[[47]](#cite_note-EqS2E6-47)
* Irving Metzman as Sterno (7 episodes). Another Company man, specializing in computers and finance. He is often seen eating fast food, which generally annoys McCall's more refined tastes. Sterno is introduced in season one in "Back Home," eating his own invention; "Creamy Pork Puppy" (cream cheese and sauerkraut on a hotdog).[[48]](#cite_note-EqS1E13-48) Sterno helps McCall in three more season one episodes,[[49]](#cite_note-EqS1E15-49)[[42]](#cite_note-EqS1E16-42)[[50]](#cite_note-EqS1E21-50) twice in season three, "The Mystery of Manon" (Parts 1 & 2),[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9) and is last seen in the season four premiere.[[40]](#cite_note-EqS4E1-40)
* [Steven Williams](/wiki/Steven_Williams "Steven Williams") as Lt. Jefferson Burnett (6 episodes). An NYPD detective from the 83rd Precinct[[51]](#cite_note-EqS1E1-51) who is aware of McCall's past and is initially distrustful of him. Burnett is introduced from the start in the series premiere "The Equalizer," and appears five more times in season one, with his last in "Mama's Boy."[[12]](#cite_note-EqS1E2-12)[[52]](#cite_note-EqS1E3-52)[[15]](#cite_note-EqS1E5-15)[[53]](#cite_note-EqS1E6-53)[[54]](#cite_note-EqS1E9-54)
* [Robert Joy](/wiki/Robert_Joy "Robert Joy") as Jacob Stock (5 episodes). A Company operative who botches his first assignment with McCall, but manages to redeem himself in season one "The Defector."[[52]](#cite_note-EqS1E3-52) He occasionally resurfaces to assist McCall in the second and fourth seasons.[[55]](#cite_note-EqS2E1-55)[[56]](#cite_note-EqS4E14-56)[[57]](#cite_note-EqS4E19-57)[[58]](#cite_note-EqS4E20-58)
* Ken Solarino as Jeremy (5 episodes). *Pete O'Phelan's* bartender introduced in season three, "Christmas Presence."[[30]](#cite_note-EqS3E11-30)[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9)[[59]](#cite_note-EqS4E15-59) His appearance in season four "The Visitation" is not credited.[[60]](#cite_note-EqS4E9-60) He is last seen in "Prisoners of Conscience" credited as Kenneth Solarino.[[10]](#cite_note-EqS4E17-10) Solarino previously played Riegert's bodyguard Rick in "Wash Up."[[42]](#cite_note-EqS1E16-42)
* [Eddie Jones](/wiki/Eddie_Jones_(actor) "Eddie Jones (actor)") as Lt. Brannigan (4 episodes). An NYPD detective introduced in season four "The Visitation,"[[60]](#cite_note-EqS4E9-60) and last seen in "Heart of Justice."[[16]](#cite_note-EqS4E10-16)[[61]](#cite_note-EqS4E12-61)[[57]](#cite_note-EqS4E19-57) Jones had previously appeared as Mr. Winslow[[46]](#cite_note-EqS2E4-46) in season two before his new role.
* [Melissa Sue Anderson](/wiki/Melissa_Sue_Anderson "Melissa Sue Anderson") as Yvette Marcel (4 episodes). The daughter of McCall's former lover Manon. She believes that Phillipe Marcel is her natural father, but later discovers she is not. McCall keeps this to himself, telling Phillipe that he must be the one to tell her. Phillipe does tell her after being fatally wounded by an old enemy. Yvette is introduced in season two "Memories of Manon,"[[62]](#cite_note-EqS2E15-62)[[63]](#cite_note-EqS2E16-63) and is last seen in season three, "The Mystery of Manon."[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9)[[64]](#cite_note-EqS3E17-64) Anderson is the real-life wife of series co-creator Michael Sloan.
* [Christopher Murney](/wiki/Christopher_Murney "Christopher Murney") as Rudy Bagler (3 episodes). An Agency reject, con-artist, and general nuisance, but the best network man they have. McCall saves him from thugs in the season three episode "Blood & Wine."[[65]](#cite_note-EqS3E1-65) His last appearance is in "Shadow Play."[[20]](#cite_note-EqS3E8-20)
* [Joe Morton](/wiki/Joe_Morton "Joe Morton") as Carter Brock (3 episodes). "Brockie," as McCall calls him, is a Vietnam veteran and explosives expert who is introduced in season four "The Sins of Our Fathers," and McCall is not pleased with the type of people he is working for. Brock returns in "Time Present, Time Past"[[66]](#cite_note-EqS4E16-66) and is last seen in the final series episode, "Suicide Squad."[[67]](#cite_note-EqS4E22-67) Morton had previously appeared as the villain Slate[[68]](#cite_note-EqS2E22-68) at the end of season two before his new role.
* [Ray Baker](/wiki/Ray_Baker_(actor) "Ray Baker (actor)") as Dana Caldrin (3 episodes). An agent of The Company who occasionally aids McCall in his investigations. Jovial, generous and good-hearted, he is nonetheless an expert in the seedier side of New York life with a comprehensive knowledge of the city's sex industry. Dana is introduced in "The Lock Box" to save a teenage girl from trafficking.[[41]](#cite_note-EqS1E4-41) In "Desperately" he helps identify a hit-man.[[69]](#cite_note-EqS1E11-69) His last appearances is in "Dead Drop" as part of a team McCall uses to track suspects.[[49]](#cite_note-EqS1E15-49)
* [Earl Hindman](/wiki/Earl_Hindman "Earl Hindman") as Lt. Elmer (3 episodes). An NYPD detective at the 74th precinct[[70]](#cite_note-EqS2E19-70) in the second season. Starting with a brief appearance in the premiere episode "Prelude,"[[55]](#cite_note-EqS2E1-55) his next appearances is in "The Cup,"[[14]](#cite_note-EqS2E10-14) and last in "Coal Black Soul."[[70]](#cite_note-EqS2E19-70) Hindman had previously appeared as Findlay in "Breakpoint"[[71]](#cite_note-EqS1E19-71) in season one before his new role.
* [Austin Pendleton](/wiki/Austin_Pendleton "Austin Pendleton") as Jonah (3 episodes). A computer expert who helps McCall in seasons two and four.[[47]](#cite_note-EqS2E6-47)[[72]](#cite_note-EqS2E17-72)[[73]](#cite_note-EqS4E8-73) In season three, Jonah hacks "[Ma Bell](/wiki/Ma_Bell "Ma Bell")" off-screen; Pendleton does not actually appear in the episode.[[74]](#cite_note-EqS3E4-74)
* A. L. Sheppard as Detective Kelly (3 episodes). An NYPD detective in the fourth season.[[26]](#cite_note-EqS4E13-26)[[27]](#cite_note-EqS4E18-27)[[18]](#cite_note-EqS4E21-18) Sheppard previously appeared as an unnamed Forensic Detective in an earlier season four episode.[[25]](#cite_note-EqS4E7-25)
* [Charles Cioffi](/wiki/Charles_Cioffi "Charles Cioffi") as Lt. Kramer (3 episodes). An NYPD detective introduced in season two "Counterfire,"[[75]](#cite_note-EqS2E7-75)[[76]](#cite_note-EqS2E12-76) and last seen in season three "Suspicion of Innocence."[[77]](#cite_note-EqS3E3-77)
* [Yvonne Wilder](/wiki/Yvonne_Wilder "Yvonne Wilder") as Lettie (3 episodes). McCall's housekeeper and mother of two boys, Jorge and Ishmael, who occasionally impact McCall's missions. Lettie is introduced in back-to-back season one episodes, "Wash Up" and "Torn,"[[42]](#cite_note-EqS1E16-42)[[43]](#cite_note-EqS1E17-43) and last seen in season four, "The Sins of Our Fathers."[[73]](#cite_note-EqS4E8-73)
* [Anthony Zerbe](/wiki/Anthony_Zerbe "Anthony Zerbe") as Phillipe Marcel (3 episodes). A Canadian Police Inspector from Quebec and father to Yvette, who is the daughter of Manon, a Company agent and McCall's former lover. Phillipe is introduced in Memories of Manon,[[62]](#cite_note-EqS2E15-62)[[63]](#cite_note-EqS2E16-63) and is last see in season three, "The Mystery of Manon (Part 1)."[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9)
* [Saul Rubinek](/wiki/Saul_Rubinek "Saul Rubinek") as Jason Masur (3 episodes). A Company bureaucrat in season one who wants to push Control out of power and would like to "retire" McCall and other "old-guard" agents. He is introduced in "The Distant Fire" trying to capture a former operative who has turned freelance. McCall interferes, so Masur wants him taken out. When a subordinate protests, Masur declares, "There is no 'They,' I am the 'They.' To hell with Control."[[78]](#cite_note-EqS1E8-78) Masur returns in "Dead Drop" where he threatens to fire all of McCall's Company contacts.[[49]](#cite_note-EqS1E15-49) Later in "Torn," Masur hires McCall to capture an agent named Brian, who betrayed him years before. It is Rubinek's last appearance.[[43]](#cite_note-EqS1E17-43)
* [Maurice Hines](/wiki/Maurice_Hines "Maurice Hines") as Billie Bump (2 episodes). A Yale lawyer, Agency operative, and McCall's personal trainer introduced in the second season episode "Memories of Manon: Part 1"[[62]](#cite_note-EqS2E15-62) for an operation of "diversion and psychological warfare." His last appearance is in season three, "Shadow Play."[[20]](#cite_note-EqS3E8-20)
* [Leonardo Cimino](/wiki/Leonardo_Cimino "Leonardo Cimino") as Doctor Phil Molinari (2 episodes). A doctor in the Department of Health, Bureau of Contagious Diseases in season four, who helps the Medical Examiner and Dr. Lauren Demeter (Jenny Agutter) contain an outbreak in "The Visitation"[[60]](#cite_note-EqS4E9-60) and last seen in "17 Zebra."[[56]](#cite_note-EqS4E14-56) Cimino had previously appeared as mobster Thomas Marley Sr., the incarcerated father of Vincent D'Onofrio's character, in season two before his new role.[[75]](#cite_note-EqS2E7-75)
* [Jack Gilpin](/wiki/Jack_Gilpin "Jack Gilpin") as Harvey (2 episodes). A talkative pharmacy delivery man that tries to sell useless items and information to McCall, in "Desperately"[[69]](#cite_note-EqS1E11-69) and "Tip on a Sure Thing."[[79]](#cite_note-EqS2E9-79)
* [Lori Loughlin](/wiki/Lori_Loughlin "Lori Loughlin") as Jenny Morrow (2 episodes). The daughter of American journalist Frank Morrow who was kidnapped in season two, "Prelude"[[55]](#cite_note-EqS2E1-55) and later appears in "First Light"[[80]](#cite_note-EqS2E20-80) where she and Scott McCall date.
* [Cleavant Derricks](/wiki/Cleavant_Derricks_(actor) "Cleavant Derricks (actor)") as Sonny Raines (2 episodes). An Agency operative who works for Control, but is always in danger of losing his job because of his gambling addiction. McCall pays his debt to trade for a favor in "Joyride,"[[46]](#cite_note-EqS2E4-46) and utilizes his expertise in "Tip on a Sure Thing."[[79]](#cite_note-EqS2E9-79)
* [John Bedford Lloyd](/wiki/John_Bedford_Lloyd "John Bedford Lloyd") as D.A. Francis Scanlon (2 episodes). A New York District Attorney in season three that pays back a favor to McCall in "Video Games"[[32]](#cite_note-EqS3E14-32) and provides insight about a murderer in "Target of Choice."[[39]](#cite_note-EqS3E21-39)
* [Roma Maffia](/wiki/Roma_Maffia "Roma Maffia") as Sindee (2 episodes). Dana's hooker acquaintance who first appears in "Desperately"[[69]](#cite_note-EqS1E11-69) to give McCall information about a hit-man. She helps Dana with intelligence again in "Dead Drop."[[49]](#cite_note-EqS1E15-49)
* [Fred Williamson](/wiki/Fred_Williamson "Fred Williamson") as Lt. Mason Warren (2 episodes). An NYPD detective at the 75th precinct[[49]](#cite_note-EqS1E15-49) in the first season, who assists McCall. He appears in back-to-back episodes, "Reign of Terror"[[81]](#cite_note-EqS1E12-81) and "Back Home."[[48]](#cite_note-EqS1E13-48)
* Robert Blumenfeld as Wilhite (2 episodes). A [U.S. Treasury Department](/wiki/U.S._Treasury_Department "U.S. Treasury Department") [bank examiner](/wiki/Bank_examiner "Bank examiner") who helps McCall in financial inquiries with his talent; "a sixth sense of all the dirt people hide in their books. If I wasn't a Bank Examiner, I'd be a Detective."[[48]](#cite_note-EqS1E13-48) Introduced in season one in "Back Home," Wilhite investigates a bank owner who is also a landlord.[[48]](#cite_note-EqS1E13-48) In season two, Wilhite is kept busy by the [IRS](/wiki/IRS "IRS"), and checks if a business owner has "cooked" his books in "A Community of Civilized Men."[[82]](#cite_note-EqS2E3-82)
* [Wendell Pierce](/wiki/Wendell_Pierce "Wendell Pierce") as Dr. Cameron Wolff (2 episodes). A man "with a social conscience" and McCall's friend, who runs a neighborhood free clinic with a specialty in psychology. He is introduced giving his professional opinion in the season four premier, "The Last Campaign"[[40]](#cite_note-EqS4E1-40) and appears again in "Starfire."[[59]](#cite_note-EqS4E15-59)
* [Harsh Nayyar](/wiki/Harsh_Nayyar "Harsh Nayyar") as a Medical Examiner (2 episodes). An NYC coroner in season four, who performs an autopsy in "Sea of Fire"[[22]](#cite_note-EqS4E2-22) to graphically teach gang members about death. His last appearance is in "The Visitation."[[60]](#cite_note-EqS4E9-60)

#### Musicians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Musicians")]

[Stewart Copeland](/wiki/Stewart_Copeland "Stewart Copeland"), who composed the show's theme song and much of its music, made a cameo as a pickpocket in "Re-Entry." In the same episode, [David Johansen](/wiki/David_Johansen "David Johansen") (AKA Buster Poindexter), the lead singer of the [proto-punk](/wiki/Proto-punk "Proto-punk") band the [New York Dolls](/wiki/New_York_Dolls "New York Dolls"), plays a thug named Garnet, who abducts [John Goodman](/wiki/John_Goodman "John Goodman")'s character at gunpoint.[[68]](#cite_note-EqS2E22-68) [Adam Ant](/wiki/Adam_Ant "Adam Ant") played a vile villain in "The Lock Box."[[41]](#cite_note-EqS1E4-41) In "Bump and Run," [Meat Loaf](/wiki/Meat_Loaf "Meat Loaf") makes an appearance as Sugar Fly Simon, a dealer of illegal guns.[[86]](#cite_note-EqS1E10-86) [Adam "Ad-Rock" Horovitz](/wiki/Ad-Rock "Ad-Rock") of the [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") made a rare acting appearance along with [Alex Winter](/wiki/Alex_Winter "Alex Winter") in "Mama's Boy."[[54]](#cite_note-EqS1E9-54) Singer [Vitamin C](/wiki/Vitamin_C_(singer) "Vitamin C (singer)"), who is credited under her real name Colleen Ann Fitzpatrick, appears as a hostage in "Last Call"[[37]](#cite_note-EqS3E19-37) and as a singer in "[Eighteen with a Bullet](/wiki/List_of_The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)_episodes#ep70 "List of The Equalizer (1985 TV series) episodes")" who performs despite being manipulated by her dangerous manager, played by [Bruce Payne](/wiki/Bruce_Payne "Bruce Payne").[[87]](#cite_note-EqS4E4-87) In "Nocturne", the [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") singing duo, [Ashford & Simpson](/wiki/Ashford_%26_Simpson "Ashford & Simpson") perform their 1986 #4 hit single "Count Your Blessings" from their *Real Love* album.[[45]](#cite_note-EqS2E2-45) Broadway baritone singer [Bruce Hubbard](/wiki/Bruce_Hubbard "Bruce Hubbard") plays a club Manager in "Solo,"[[72]](#cite_note-EqS2E17-72) and Detective Warren in "Race Traitors" alongside singer/guitarist [Michael Cerveris](/wiki/Michael_Cerveris "Michael Cerveris") portraying Nick Kaminsky, who gets caught up with a group of skinheads and an Aryan Leader played by [The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") founder [John Cale](/wiki/John_Cale "John Cale").[[58]](#cite_note-EqS4E20-58) Singer [Karen Akers](/wiki/Karen_Akers "Karen Akers") had a supporting role in "China Rain" as Cynthia, a nightclub "Head of Personal Relations."[[12]](#cite_note-EqS1E2-12) [Jasmine Guy](/wiki/Jasmine_Guy "Jasmine Guy"), in her first credited on-screen role, plays Gloria in "Out of the Past."[[88]](#cite_note-EqS1E14-88) In the episode "Inner View,"[[28]](#cite_note-EqS3E9-28) singer [Paul Hipp](/wiki/Paul_Hipp "Paul Hipp") plays Jarret, the lead singer of Archon, baritone singer [Terrence Mann](/wiki/Terrence_Mann "Terrence Mann") is a villain called "Shadow Man", and multi-instrumentalist [Tim Cappello](/wiki/Tim_Cappello "Tim Cappello") appears as an Archon roadie. Cappello also plays an adult shopkeeper in "A Place to Stay."[[17]](#cite_note-EqS2E18-17)[[83]](#cite_note-MeTVcrazy-83)[[84]](#cite_note-Nine18-84)

#### News and media personalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: News and media personalities")]

Notable news and talk show hosts, disc jockeys, comedians, and other personalities from the television and radio include:

* [Sally Jesse Raphael](/wiki/Sally_Jesse_Raphael "Sally Jesse Raphael") and [Bobby Rivers](/wiki/Bobby_Rivers "Bobby Rivers") both interview a gun-control advocate, Sylvia Thorton, played by [Barbara Williams](/wiki/Barbara_Williams_(actress) "Barbara Williams (actress)") in "Making of a Martyr."[[25]](#cite_note-EqS4E7-25)
* New York [WNBC](/wiki/WNBC "WNBC") news anchor [Roger Grimsby](/wiki/Roger_Grimsby "Roger Grimsby") reports on the assault of a night watchman at the zoo, and an escaped timber wolf roaming Manhattan in "The Mystery of Manon" (Part 1)."[[9]](#cite_note-EqS3E16-9)
* [Warner Wolf](/wiki/Warner_Wolf "Warner Wolf") appears on McCall's TV set commenting on the current horse races in "Tip on a Sure Thing."[[79]](#cite_note-EqS2E9-79)
* New York media personality [Donna Fiducia](/wiki/Donna_Fiducia "Donna Fiducia") appears as newscaster Diane Waters in "The Cup."[[14]](#cite_note-EqS2E10-14)
* Sheila Stainback of *[INN / USA Tonight](/wiki/Independent_Network_News_(TV_program) "Independent Network News (TV program)")* and of [WPIX](/wiki/WPIX "WPIX") *New York Tonight* made cameos as an Anchorperson in "Blood & Wine,"[[65]](#cite_note-EqS3E1-65) as herself in "Inner View,"[[28]](#cite_note-EqS3E9-28) and as a Newscaster in "Race Traitors."[[58]](#cite_note-EqS4E20-58)
* Journalist [Lycia Naff](/wiki/Lycia_Naff "Lycia Naff") plays the concerned girlfriend of Danny Winters (Christopher Collet's character) in "The Child Broker."[[31]](#cite_note-EqS3E13-31)
* [Ken Ober](/wiki/Ken_Ober "Ken Ober") plays a DJ who takes fan calls live on WZAD with Beverly Heat (Vitamin C's character) in "Eighteen with a Bullet."[[87]](#cite_note-EqS4E4-87)
* Native New Yorker comedian [Mickey Freeman](/wiki/Mickey_Freeman "Mickey Freeman") made his last television appearance in "Counterfire,"[[75]](#cite_note-EqS2E7-75) doing a lousy job in front of a tough crowd at a comedy club.
* NYC stand-up comedian [Rick Aviles](/wiki/Rick_Aviles "Rick Aviles") appeared as Scam in "A Place to Stay."[[17]](#cite_note-EqS2E18-17)

#### Stage performers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stage performers")]

The series also made good use of its New York City filming location/setting by employing actors[[85]](#cite_note-WiredCabin-85) who were appearing on Broadway in the late 1980s as guest stars. These include [Christine Baranski](/wiki/Christine_Baranski "Christine Baranski"), [Philip Bosco](/wiki/Philip_Bosco "Philip Bosco"), [Yvonne Bryceland](/wiki/Yvonne_Bryceland "Yvonne Bryceland"), [J. Smith-Cameron](/wiki/J._Smith-Cameron "J. Smith-Cameron"), [Caitlin Clarke](/wiki/Caitlin_Clarke "Caitlin Clarke"), [Kevin Conway](/wiki/Kevin_Conway_(actor) "Kevin Conway (actor)"), [Jim Dale](/wiki/Jim_Dale "Jim Dale"), [Dee Hoty](/wiki/Dee_Hoty "Dee Hoty"), Bruce Hubbard, Terrence Mann, [Frances Ruffelle](/wiki/Frances_Ruffelle "Frances Ruffelle"), [Josef Sommer](/wiki/Josef_Sommer "Josef Sommer"), and [Anne Twomey](/wiki/Anne_Twomey_(actor) "Anne Twomey (actor)").

#### Woodward colleagues and family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Woodward colleagues and family")]

Additionally, several former stage and screen co-stars of Edward Woodward appeared on the show. These included [Brian Bedford](/wiki/Brian_Bedford "Brian Bedford"),[[86]](#cite_note-EqS1E10-86) [Tammy Grimes](/wiki/Tammy_Grimes "Tammy Grimes")[[82]](#cite_note-EqS2E3-82) (real-life mother of [Amanda Plummer](/wiki/Amanda_Plummer "Amanda Plummer")), [Gwen Verdon](/wiki/Gwen_Verdon "Gwen Verdon"),[[44]](#cite_note-EqS1E18-44) [Sandy Dennis](/wiki/Sandy_Dennis "Sandy Dennis"),[[88]](#cite_note-EqS1E14-88) [Jenny Agutter](/wiki/Jenny_Agutter "Jenny Agutter"),[[60]](#cite_note-EqS4E9-60) [Shirley Knight](/wiki/Shirley_Knight "Shirley Knight"),[[66]](#cite_note-EqS4E16-66) and [Sylvia Sidney](/wiki/Sylvia_Sidney "Sylvia Sidney").[[11]](#cite_note-EqS4E11-11)

Woodward's second wife, [Michele Dotrice](/wiki/Michele_Dotrice "Michele Dotrice"), appeared as the central character in "Heartstrings".[[89]](#cite_note-EqS2E11-89) Her father, [Roy Dotrice](/wiki/Roy_Dotrice "Roy Dotrice"), also guest-starred on the show in "Trial by Ordeal."[[11]](#cite_note-EqS4E11-11) Edward Woodward's son, actor [Tim Woodward](/wiki/Tim_Woodward "Tim Woodward"), appeared as McCall's father in a flashback scene in "Prisoners of Conscience."[[10]](#cite_note-EqS4E17-10)

#### Film and Reboot guest stars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Film and Reboot guest stars")]

A few notable guest stars have gone on to appear in *The Equalizer* [films](#Film_series) and [2021 revival](#2021_television_revival_series) series.

* Neal Ben-Ari made his television debut on season one of *The Equalizer* as Male Suit in "No Conscience" (1986),[[90]](#cite_note-EqS1E20-90) and plays mobster Stanley in season three, "Something Green" (1988).[[36]](#cite_note-EqS3E15-36) In 2022, Benari played Arieh Bitton in the reboot episode, "[What Dreams May Come](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(2021_TV_series)_season_2#ep27 "The Equalizer (2021 TV series) season 2")."
* [Dennis Boutsikaris](/wiki/Dennis_Boutsikaris "Dennis Boutsikaris") played C.R. Heaton in season three, "In the Money"[[74]](#cite_note-EqS3E4-74) (1987) and played Judge Thomas Clemmens in the reboot episode, "[D.W.B.](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(2021_TV_series)_season_2#ep23 "The Equalizer (2021 TV series) season 2")" (2022).
* [Melissa Leo](/wiki/Melissa_Leo "Melissa Leo"), who guest starred as Irina Dzershinsky[[52]](#cite_note-EqS1E3-52) in the third episode of season one, "[The Defector](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)_season_1#ep3 "The Equalizer (1985 TV series) season 1")," plays Susan Plummer in all three films.
* [John Bedford Lloyd](/wiki/John_Bedford_Lloyd "John Bedford Lloyd") who played [D.A. Francis Scanlon](#Recurring) also played Howard in the reboot season two episode, "[Vox Populi](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(2021_TV_series)_season_2#ep26 "The Equalizer (2021 TV series) season 2")."

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Music")]

The show's theme music was created by composer/performer [Stewart Copeland](/wiki/Stewart_Copeland "Stewart Copeland"). The track is called "Busy Equalizing". An extended version appears on his album *The Equalizer and Other Cliff Hangers.*

Six episodes in the 1988 season were scored by [Joseph Conlan](/wiki/Joseph_Conlan "Joseph Conlan") and the final season was scored by [Cameron Allan](/wiki/Cameron_Allan "Cameron Allan"). Other episodes were scored by [John Cacavas](/wiki/John_Cacavas "John Cacavas").

The show ran for four seasons of 22 episodes each. It was initially renewed for a fifth season (causing Keith Szarabajka to turn down a role on *[Midnight Caller](/wiki/Midnight_Caller "Midnight Caller")*). However, the show was later canceled due to a row between CBS and Universal Studios over the renewal of *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*.[[93]](#cite_note-DVDTcomcol-93)

In *The Story of The Equalizer*, created for the DVD box set, executive producer Coleman Luck also stated that Universal requested a script for a [crossover episode](/wiki/Crossover_episode "Crossover episode") with *[Magnum, P.I.](/wiki/Magnum,_P.I. "Magnum, P.I.")* despite the objections of the *Equalizer* crew as its dark tone was much different than *Magnum*'s much lighter and sunnier style. Ultimately the episode was never filmed, but its script was adapted and changed, ultimately airing as the episode "Beyond Control".

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Critical response")]

After the broadcast of the first episode in September 1985, the show received mixed reviews. Critic [Tom Shales](/wiki/Tom_Shales "Tom Shales") wrote in *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* that "to judge from this very attractively atmospheric premiere, [the show] could become a welcome guest in many an American home".[[106]](#cite_note-106) However, for *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")*, [Jeff Jarvis](/wiki/Jeff_Jarvis "Jeff Jarvis") wrote, "The plot’s confusing and the show’s as erratic as a pacemaker on low batteries".[[107]](#cite_note-107)

## Localizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Localizations")]

The series was translated into [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") for broadcast through South Africa's [SABC](/wiki/SABC "SABC") as *Die laaste uitweg*.

The series was broadcast throughout the Arab World with Arabic subtitles during the 1990s, like in Egypt through [Channel 2](/wiki/Television_in_Egypt "Television in Egypt") and Saudi Arabia through [Saudi 2](/wiki/Saudi_2 "Saudi 2").[[112]](#cite_note-112)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Home media")]

On February 12, 2008, [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Home_Entertainment "Universal Studios Home Entertainment") released Season 1 of *The Equalizer* on [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") in Region 1 (US only).[[113]](#cite_note-SoDVDs1-113)

On May 15, 2013, it was announced that [Visual Entertainment](/wiki/Visual_Entertainment "Visual Entertainment") had acquired the rights to the series in Region 1.[[114]](#cite_note-SoDVDplan-114) They subsequently released season 2 on DVD on August 26, 2014.[[115]](#cite_note-SoDVDs3-115)[[116]](#cite_note-SoDVDs2-116) Season 3 was released on October 25, 2014, followed by season 4 on November 24, 2014.

[Visual Entertainment](/wiki/Visual_Entertainment "Visual Entertainment") also released a limited edition complete series set on DVD on August 19, 2014.[[117]](#cite_note-VisualEntertainment.tv-117) *The Equalizer Complete Collection Limited Edition* set contains all 88 episodes plus 12 hours of bonus content including *[CI5: The New Professionals](/wiki/CI5:_The_New_Professionals "CI5: The New Professionals")*; the last film ever done by Edward Woodward, *A Congregation of Ghosts*; and *The Story of The Equalizer* featuring interviews with cast and crew.[[117]](#cite_note-VisualEntertainment.tv-117)

In Region 2, Universal Playback UK released season 1 on DVD on April 21, 2008. In late 2011, Fabulous Films announced that they had acquired the rights to the series. They have subsequently released seasons 2–4.[[118]](#cite_note-FabFilms-118) On May 27, 2013, Fabulous Films released *The Equalizer: The Complete Collection* on DVD.[[119]](#cite_note-FabFilmsCC-119) This 24-disc box set contains all 88 episodes of the series as well as bonus content including an all-new documentary featuring interviews with cast and crew.

Many fans have noted that the Region 1 version has had several of the originally used songs replaced. In fact nearly 50 percent of all music apart from the theme and scoring by Stewart Copeland has been replaced by "covers" due to expired music licensing agreements and the costs which would be incurred in the US to have the licensing renewed. In contrast, the Region 2 version contains all of the original music intact.

In Region 4, Umbrella Entertainment has released all four seasons on DVD in Australia.[[120]](#cite_note-Umbrel4-120)

## Adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Adaptations")]

### Film series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Film series")]

Main articles: [The Equalizer (film)](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(film) "The Equalizer (film)"), [The Equalizer 2](/wiki/The_Equalizer_2 "The Equalizer 2"), and [The Equalizer 3](/wiki/The_Equalizer_3 "The Equalizer 3")

A film starring [Denzel Washington](/wiki/Denzel_Washington "Denzel Washington") in the title role, very loosely based upon the series, was released in September 2014. It was followed by a sequel, *[The Equalizer 2](/wiki/The_Equalizer_2 "The Equalizer 2")*, which was released on July 20, 2018. A third film, titled *[The Equalizer 3](/wiki/The_Equalizer_3 "The Equalizer 3")*, with Washington and director [Antoine Fuqua](/wiki/Antoine_Fuqua "Antoine Fuqua") returning[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122) was released on September 1, 2023.

### 2021 television revival series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2021 television revival series")]

Main article: [The Equalizer (2021 TV series)](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(2021_TV_series) "The Equalizer (2021 TV series)")

In November 2019, CBS announced that a reboot was in development with [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah") in the lead role as Robyn McCall. [Andrew Marlowe](/wiki/Andrew_Marlowe "Andrew Marlowe") and [Terri Miller](/wiki/Terri_Edda_Miller "Terri Edda Miller") were named as showrunners with Latifah herself as an executive producer.[[123]](#cite_note-123) On January 27, 2020, CBS issued a pilot order for the new version.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

The series was among the 14 pilots ordered by CBS in February 2020 and was fast tracked to series the following March, as they are unable to film their pilots where [Universal Television](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television") was shut down because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[125]](#cite_note-125)

On May 8, 2020, CBS picked up the series[[126]](#cite_note-126) and they added [Chris Noth](/wiki/Chris_Noth "Chris Noth") as William Bishop, a quirky ex-CIA director who is the opposite of Latifah.[[127]](#cite_note-127) It premiered on February 7, 2021, after [Super Bowl LV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LV "Super Bowl LV").[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Literature")]

A collection of novels featuring Robert McCall has been written by the original co-creator Michael Sloan. The first is simply entitled *The Equalizer* published in 2014, followed by *Killed in Action: An Equalizer Novel*, which was released in 2018. The novels are a modern reimagining of the original series and focus on McCall leaving The Company and eventually becoming a private investigator in New York. It also features a number of original recurring characters from the television series such as Mickey Kostmayer, Control, and Scott McCall. A third novel, *Equalizer: Requiem* was released in 2020.
